findings.topic: signing call vibe and red flags
findings.title: Treat the signing call as a two-way interview with hard non-negotiables
findings.claim: Operators sign more durable creators by qualifying them on the call against work-ethic and commitment non-negotiables instead of signing whoever replies.
findings.what_they_do: Opens the call human-first (camera on, 'how are you', hobbies) to break barriers, then qualifies: rejects short-term-money motives and 'overly involved' creators, requires committed daily hours and a fixed content/live cadence, and walks away if the vibe or non-negotiables fail.
findings.vs_us: Palm acquisition is Josh-DM-led with no documented signing-call qualification framework (§6 THIN); this is a build-toward script Palm lacks.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a documented signing-call SOP: human-first rapport, explicit work-ethic/cadence non-negotiables, and willingness to reject misaligned creators.

sources.quote: work ethic trumps everything
have non-negotiables and have some respect for yourself
we prefer it's between 5 and six because then that allows a lot more content to be made

findings.title: Never promise income numbers; sell yourself and let results speak
findings.claim: Promising creators specific income figures during acquisition breaks trust when results fall short, so operators should sell themselves and process instead.
findings.what_they_do: Refuses to quote first-month dollar figures (e.g. won't say 'I'll make you 50k'), declines to discuss other models' numbers, and instead frames the pitch around being a good long-term partner whose results will speak for themselves.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no written acquisition messaging discipline (§6 THIN); this guards a first-month-churn risk Palm has no codified position on.
findings.recommendation: Make 'never promise income numbers; sell the partnership and let results speak' an explicit rule in Palm's onboarding playbook.

sources.quote: your words have power and by the first month if you don't make that 50k... she's going to leave
I'm not going to discuss numbers. I'm here to sell myself as a person

findings.title: Source creators from OF-video comment sections and push to a call
findings.claim: Creator acquisition works by finding help-seeking creators in social comment sections, outreaching across every platform with a curiosity-driven message, and pushing for a quick call rather than closing over DMs.
findings.what_they_do: Searches TikTok for OnlyFans-related videos and mines the comment sections for girls asking for help (a signal they lack an agency), outreaches on every platform she's on with a short curiosity message ('100 fresh paying subs every single day'), and immediately pushes to a 5-minute call.
findings.vs_us: Palm acquisition is outbound-DM + referral but with no documented sourcing method, message template, or call-booking step (§6 GAP).
findings.recommendation: Codify a sourcing playbook (comment-section mining of help-seeking creators), a curiosity-led opener, multi-platform outreach, and call-booking as the CTA.

sources.quote: within the comment section will be girls doing Only Fans asking for help
you basically just want to push for a call straight away

findings.title: Prefer percentage deals with Western creators over LATAM salary models
findings.claim: Percentage splits with US/UK/Australian creators outperform paying monthly salaries to lower-cost-region creators because Western audiences spend more and the model carries no upfront cost.
findings.what_they_do: Moved off salaried Colombian/Argentinian models (high overhead, hard to reach US spenders, recurring raise pressure) to zero-upfront percentage deals (e.g. 50/50) with US/UK/AU creators whose audiences actually pay premium PPV prices.
findings.vs_us: Palm sets commission per-creator at onboarding but has no written split/model rationale (§7 GAP); this benchmarks Palm's percentage approach against the salary alternative.
findings.recommendation: Document Palm's percentage-over-salary rationale and Western-creator targeting as the codified pricing/commission position.

sources.quote: going from zero to 10K with a salary model is like a billion times harder than with a percentage model from the west
If it's a 50/50, it's pretty much based on what you make her. She takes half

findings.title: Charge no upfront or hidden fees; earn only on a partnership split
findings.claim: Legitimate agencies take no money upfront and no hidden fees, charging only via the revenue split, which is itself a trust differentiator at signing.
findings.what_they_do: Takes nothing upfront and no hidden fees, contrasting against agencies that make creators 'buy their way in'; frames the relationship as partners not assets and lays all terms out from the beginning so they never change.
findings.vs_us: Palm produces a contract PDF at onboarding but has no documented fee-structure stance (§7 GAP); this is a concrete trust-positioning rule to adopt.
findings.recommendation: Codify 'no upfront, no hidden fees, partner-not-asset' as explicit Palm acquisition trust positioning.

sources.quote: nothing up front... I've been approached by a lot of other agencies and they all wanted me to buy my way into the agency
we really strive on being partners, you know, and not just treating you like an asset

findings.title: Train chatters on a fixed funnel: questions, imposition, pull-push, guilt trips
findings.claim: DM revenue depends on running every fan through a fixed pre-sale framework (data-collection questions, position check, pull-push flirting, then scripted PPV with guilt-trip fallbacks) rather than letting chatters spam PPVs.
findings.what_they_do: Trains new chatters on six new-subscriber questions (name, age, location, work, preferences, kinks) logged to notes, an 'imposition' check that the fan is in a buyable situation, a 'pull-push' flirting loop, then the PPV script, with pre-written guilt-trip messages for each failure point; reports a 75-80% unlock rate.
findings.vs_us: Palm outsources chatting with no internal SOP/scripts/funnel (§8 OUTSOURCED); this is direct build-toward intelligence for the in-house and AI chat roadmap.
findings.recommendation: Capture this questions→imposition→pull-push→script→guilt-trip framework as the backbone SOP for Palm's future in-house chat team and as AI-chat training material.

sources.quote: that's why my chatter conversions are 75 to 80% plus
you don't want to let the fan see the sales knife
pull fan in with a flirty comment... and then push them away with a generic question

findings.title: Build a fan profile through scripted questions before any PPV
findings.claim: Selling is most effective when chatters first collect structured fan data (name, location, age, job, kinks) to gauge spending power and build connection, instead of spamming PPVs on entry.
findings.what_they_do: Uses systemized new-subscriber scripts with pre-written replies so every chatter sounds identical to the model, collects data to estimate purchasing power (e.g. country/job), records it in per-fan notes, and only escalates to a PPV ladder that builds up to ~$200.
findings.vs_us: Palm has a Fan Tracker CRM and whale data (§9 PARTIAL) but no chatting scripts or data-capture SOP (§8 OUTSOURCED).
findings.recommendation: Pair Palm's fan CRM with a scripted data-collection intake and a build-up PPV ladder for the in-house/AI chat build.

sources.quote: what's going to make you money is data, right? Data collection
you can get the scripts all the way up to $200

findings.title: Automate fan re-engagement so chatters focus only on inbound
findings.claim: Revenue scales when automated, rotating 'smart message' sequences trigger on fan activity to remove human error and free chatters to work only inbound conversations.
findings.what_they_do: Sets up Infloww smart-message 'nudge' collections that fire when a fan comes online (after a delay), rotates ~10 collections every two weeks so messages feel fresh, auto-unsends ignored sequences, and stops the sequence once the fan replies—so chatters never do manual outreach.
findings.vs_us: Palm's tech spine has Publer scheduling but no fan-CRM-driven re-engagement automation tied to chatting (§8/§9 PARTIAL).
findings.recommendation: Plan a smart-message-style automated re-engagement layer (activity-triggered, rotating, auto-unsend) for Palm's future fan CRM / AI-chat stack.

sources.quote: you want to completely remove human error from chatters
this feature has made me hundreds of thousands of dollars for all my models

findings.title: Run the agency on a defined six-tool stack
findings.claim: A six-figure operation runs on a defined low-cost software stack: fan CRM, video-call tool, deep-link redirect, editor, anti-detect browser, and Telegram for coordination.
findings.what_they_do: Uses Infloww (fan CRM/chatting/scripts, priced as a capped revenue percentage), Google Meet (model calls), Bouncy.ai deep links to hide OF URLs from Meta/Snap, CapCut (editing), Dolphin Anty anti-detect browser + proxies for Reddit, and Telegram with per-topic subgroups to run the whole team.
findings.vs_us: Palm already has a strong spine (Publer, Airtable, Cloudflare, Telegram routing §0/§5) but lacks a fan-CRM equivalent to Infloww and a deep-link/anti-detect layer.
findings.recommendation: Map each function to Palm's stack; close the fan-CRM gap (Infloww-style) and evaluate deep-link redirects for link-in-bio safety. Palm already covers Telegram and scheduling.

sources.quote: what we do is we do this thing called a deep link
the most important one... will be Telegram

findings.title: Hire cheap offshore chatters but train them intensively and systemize the work
findings.claim: The cost-effective chatting model is hiring low-cost offshore chatters and de-risking them through heavy recurring training plus fully systemized scripts so output doesn't depend on their prior experience.
findings.what_they_do: Hires Filipino chatters at ~$2-3/hour via OnlineJobs.ph, distrusts 'experienced' chatters who spam PPVs, and trains them personally 4-5 times a week (~20-30 min) on his own systems so every chatter sounds identical and 'can't mess up'.
findings.vs_us: Palm has no in-house chatters yet (§8) and no staffing/training model documented (§10 THIN); this informs the future in-house chat team's hiring and training design.
findings.recommendation: Plan Palm's in-house chat team around systemized scripts + intensive recurring training rather than relying on hires' prior experience.

sources.quote: Go to online jobs.ph. Get yourself a Filipino chatter. Two $3 per hour
teaching your chatters at least three times per week for 30 minutes a day minimum

findings.topic: systemize then scale one creator at a time
findings.title: Systemize day-one onboarding and perfect one creator before scaling
findings.claim: Reaching the first 10k/month comes from running the business as a real systemized operation—day-one onboarding, scripts, chatting, marketing all in place—and perfecting one creator before adding the next.
findings.what_they_do: Insists onboarding (Drive, content sheet/niche, scripts) be completed on day one not stretched over a week, gets Infloww scripts and smart messages set up before a chatter starts, focuses one marketing channel, and only scales to the next creator once one is fully systemized.
findings.vs_us: Palm is multi-creator past this stage, but the 'systemize before you scale' discipline maps onto Palm's THIN/GAP areas (acquisition §6, pricing §7, retention §9) that aren't yet documented.
findings.recommendation: Apply 'document and systemize before delegating/scaling' to close Palm's THIN areas before further growth.

sources.quote: It needs to all be done on day one
Perfect that for one model. Scale to the next

findings.title: Keep the profile wall teasing, never explicit, to preserve PPV value
findings.claim: Profile/wall content should tease (lingerie, not nudity) so curiosity—and willingness to pay for PPV—stays intact.
findings.what_they_do: Refuses to put nudes or explicit content on the OF wall, reasoning that if the fan sees everything for free or at subscription price there is nothing left to sell; keeps the wall at lingerie-level to sustain curiosity.
findings.vs_us: Palm is strong on social top-of-funnel content (§1 MATURE) but thin on OF-vault/PPV content strategy (§2 THIN); this is a concrete vault-merchandising rule.
findings.recommendation: Adopt a 'tease-not-reveal on the wall' rule as part of Palm's OF-vault/PPV content guidance.

sources.quote: do not put any nudes... why is the fan going to spend any money when he's basically seen all of that for free

findings.title: Real models beat AI on connection, customs and platform access
findings.claim: AI personas underperform real creators because most OFM revenue comes from personal connection and custom content, and AI is pushed off OnlyFans onto less reliable platforms.
findings.what_they_do: Argues AI-model courses are 'selling shovels in a gold rush', that the real money is in fans believing they're talking to a real girl and in customs AI can't do, that OnlyFans won't host AI so you're forced to Fanvue (which pays out unreliably), and that referrals from happy real creators are the best growth engine.
findings.vs_us: Tempers Palm's AI-persona thesis (§0/§3): Palm's AI personas still funnel real social connection to OF, but this flags the customs/whale and platform-access limits and the Fanvue payout risk for any future AI-creator line.
findings.recommendation: Keep Palm's AI personas as a social/scaling lever while concentrating customs/whale monetization on real creators; treat Fanvue-class platforms and payout reliability as explicit risks for any AI-creator expansion.

sources.quote: they are all pretty much trying to sell you shovels in a gold rush
if you don't get paid, you don't get paid

findings.title: An AI chatbot can replace much of the chat team at a fraction of the cost
findings.claim: A well-configured AI chatting bot can handle 24/7 fan conversations and PPV upsells at a fraction of a chat team's cost, but only if it is carefully set up and trained.
findings.what_they_do: Uses Super Creator's 'Izzy' AI chatbot (~$200-300/month vs a full chatting team), configured with extensive persona data, slang, pricing strategy, and trained on rapport/texting/selling scenarios; warns that a bad setup just creates 'a shitty chatter'.
findings.vs_us: Directly maps to Palm's active AI-chatting project (§8 build-toward): defines a concrete tool pattern and the heavy-configuration requirement.
findings.recommendation: Evaluate a configurable AI chat bot (Super Creator-class) for Palm's AI-chatting project, budgeting for heavy persona setup/training rather than expecting plug-and-play.

sources.quote: it only costs like $2 $300 a month, which is pennies compared to how much you're forking out... on chatters
if you don't set it up correctly, you are simply going to make a shitty chatter

findings.title: Let AI sort and convert new subs; route spenders to human chatters
findings.claim: The emerging chatting meta is a hybrid where AI handles new-subscriber volume and identifies real spenders, then hands those fans to human chatters for long-term girlfriend-experience retention.
findings.what_they_do: Has AI run all new subscribers and complete the first script to separate spenders from time-wasters, then moves real spenders to a human chatter who gives a daily girlfriend experience to a small client list (~20 fans each); reports ~70% unlock rate and a single whale spending $50k in 4 months.
findings.vs_us: Defines the target architecture for Palm's AI-chatting project and future in-house team (§8 build-toward): AI for volume/sorting, humans for whale retention.
findings.recommendation: Design Palm's chat roadmap as a hybrid: AI for new-sub volume and spender-sorting, human chatters owning small VIP client lists for retention, with unlock-rate as a tracked KPI.

sources.quote: I will let the AI deal with all the new subscribers... so that it can differentiate between the actual spenders and the time wasters
we want the chatter to specifically just focus on a specific number amount of clients
